w:r.w:t: ⚠ The tenant onboarding flow is NOT atomic — partial failures leave corrupt data in the database
The current SignupService in the Java backend splits the tenant creation and user creation across two separate transaction boundaries intentionally:
TenantPersistenceService uses REQUIRES_NEW to commit tenant first, then UserCreationService creates the user in a second transaction.
To solve a Hibernate @TenantId chicken-and-egg problem: the user's tenantId FK must reference a committed tenant row.
If user creation fails after tenant commits, the system has a tenant row with no admin user, no subscription, no roles, and no permissions.
Every such broken tenant is invisible in the UI but occupies a company_code and workspace_slug slot. There is no recovery path currently.
// SignupService.java — NO @Transactional on the outer method
// Step 1: Pre-checks (not in transaction — race condition possible)
// Step 2: COMMIT 1 — tenant saved in its own transaction
// 💥 IF ANYTHING FAILS HERE — tenant is already committed
DB connection lost between tenant save and user save
Tenant row exists, no user, no subscription
Email already taken (race condition on concurrent signups)
workspace_slug collision (concurrent signup with same name)
First slug check passes, both try to insert
One succeeds fully, one gets tenant with no user
Tenant has admin user but admin cannot do anything
Tenant active but no plan assigned, quota checks fail
1.3 What Is Also Missing From The Signup Flow
But outside transaction — race condition possible
Platform TenantService does it, SignupService does not
Admin has no permissions after self-service signup
Tenant settings initialization (invoice sequence, expiry days)
Defaults exist on entity but no explicit init
Only logged if user creation succeeds
Rollback of tenant if user creation fails
Return meaningful error if partial failure
Make the entire signup flow either fully succeed or fully roll back with zero orphaned data
Solve the Hibernate @TenantId chicken-and-egg problem without splitting transactions
Create all required seed data during signup: subscription, default roles, permissions, settings
Add idempotent duplicate detection with proper conflict error messages before any DB writes
Return typed, actionable error responses for every failure mode
Write an audit log entry for every signup attempt (success and failure)
Cover the complete onboarding flow with integration tests using Testcontainers
Email verification flow (separate PRD — email service not yet integrated)
Payment processing at signup (subscription is always trial)
Admin approval of new tenants (open signup model)
Core Approach: Introduce a dedicated TenantOnboardingService that runs all steps inside ONE Saga-style coordinated flow with explicit compensating rollbacks
3.1 Why Not Just Put @Transactional On SignupService?
The standard fix would be @Transactional on the entire signup method. This fails here because of a Hibernate @TenantId constraint:
Hibernate reads TenantContext.getTenantIdentifier() to auto-set tenant_id on new entities
TenantContext is set from the JWT on existing requests — but during signup there is no JWT yet
When we call userRepository.save(user) with tenant_id from TenantContext, Hibernate resolves it at flush time
If both the tenant INSERT and user INSERT are in the same transaction, Hibernate may try to flush in an order that violates the FK constraint (users.tenant_id → tenants.id)
The solution is a two-phase approach using saveAndFlush for the tenant, then proceeding within the same outer transaction for all subsequent steps:
Check email uniqueness, workspace_slug uniqueness, company_code generation — all inside a read-only validation phase BEFORE any writes. Return 409 Conflict with field-specific error message.
Create Business/Tenant entity, call saveAndFlush() to immediately write and get generated ID. TenantContext is set to the new tenant ID.
Create TENANT_ADMIN role scoped to new tenant. Also create BUSINESS_MANAGER, SALES_STAFF, INVENTORY_MANAGER, FINANCE_MANAGER, VIEWER roles with correct permissions.
Create admin User entity with role=TENANT_ADMIN, scope=TENANT. TenantContext ensures @TenantId is set correctly. Password is BCrypt hashed.
Create Subscription row: plan=FREE/STARTER, status=TRIAL, end_date = now + 7 days.
Set invoiceSequence=1000, expiryThresholdDays=30, currency=INR on tenant entity. Update tenant status=ACTIVE.
Write SIGNUP audit entry with tenantId, userId, IP, userAgent. This is inside the same transaction.
All 6 steps commit atomically. TenantContext is cleared in finally block.

The critical insight is that saveAndFlush() writes the tenant row to the database AND returns the generated ID — all within the outer @Transactional boundary. Once the tenant row is flushed (not yet committed), subsequent INSERTs to users table can reference the tenant_id FK because they are in the same database transaction session.
Key: saveAndFlush() ≠ commit. The row is visible within the same transaction session, satisfying FK constraints. The outer @Transactional still controls the commit/rollback.
The TenantContext must be set BEFORE calling userRepository.save() so Hibernate can resolve the @TenantId. Since we have the tenant ID from saveAndFlush(), this is safe.
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users WHERE email = ? (native query, bypasses tenant filter)
409 Conflict: { field: 'ownerEmail', code: 'EMAIL_TAKEN', message: 'This email is already registered' }
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM tenants WHERE name = ? (case-insensitive)
409 Conflict: { field: 'businessName', code: 'NAME_TAKEN', message: 'A business with this name already exists' }
Auto-generated from businessName. Checked before write, not in a loop.
Auto-suffix with random 4-char code instead of looping
Auto-generated. Checked before write with retry max 5 attempts.
If 5 collisions occur, throw 500 with alert — should never happen
Must be one of: pharmacy, supermarket, retail, warehouse, restaurant
Min 8 chars, at least 1 uppercase, 1 number (validated via @Pattern)
400 Bad Request with field-level validation error
The TenantOnboardingService.onboard() method MUST be annotated with @Transactional(rollbackFor = Exception.class). The full sequence of writes is:
Tenant entity: INSERT INTO tenants + saveAndFlush()
Role entities: 6 default roles INSERT (TENANT_ADMIN, BUSINESS_MANAGER, SALES_STAFF, INVENTORY_MANAGER, FINANCE_MANAGER, VIEWER)
Role-Permission mappings: INSERT INTO role_permissions for each role
User entity: INSERT INTO users with tenant_id from step 1
Subscription entity: INSERT INTO subscriptions (TRIAL, 7 days)
Tenant update: UPDATE tenants SET status=ACTIVE, invoice_sequence=1000 WHERE id=?
If ANY of these fail, the entire transaction rolls back. The database returns to exactly the state it was in before signup was called.
Every new tenant must receive exactly these 6 roles with their permission sets:
Full access to all tenant features
view_business, create_user, view_user, update_user, create_product, view_product, update_product, stock_in, stock_out, view_reports, create_invoice
view_product, create_product, update_product, stock_in, stock_out, view_reports

Implementation note: Permissions are pre-seeded in the database by Flyway migrations or the existing SeedService. The onboarding service should look up permission IDs by key rather than hardcoding them. Cache the permission lookup if performance is a concern.
FREE (or first plan in subscription_plans where status=ACTIVE)
Current implementation uses a while loop with DB checks which is a race condition. Replace with:
// Append random suffix to avoid collision without DB query
The random 6-char suffix gives 36^6 = ~2.1 billion combinations. Slug collision is effectively impossible. Remove the retry loop. A unique constraint on workspace_slug remains as the DB safety net.
{ error: 'EMAIL_TAKEN', field: 'ownerEmail', message: 'This email is already registered' }
{ error: 'BUSINESS_NAME_TAKEN', field: 'businessName', message: 'A business with this name already exists' }
{ error: 'VALIDATION_FAILED', fields: { businessType: 'Must be one of: pharmacy, supermarket, retail, warehouse, restaurant' } }
{ error: 'VALIDATION_FAILED', fields: { adminPassword: 'Min 8 chars, 1 uppercase, 1 number' } }
{ error: 'ONBOARDING_FAILED', message: 'Signup could not be completed. Please try again.' } — log full stack trace internally
{ message, companyCode, workspaceSlug, tenantId, adminUserId, trialEndsAt }
public OnboardingResult onboard(SignupRequest req, OnboardingMeta meta) {
// Phase 3 — Write Tenant (flush immediately to get ID)
tenant = tenantRepository.saveAndFlush(tenant); // FK available in session
// Phase 4 — Set TenantContext so @TenantId resolves correctly
// Phase 5 — Seed default roles with permissions
TenantContext.clear(); // Always clear — even on exception
// validateUniqueness — throws OnboardingConflictException for each case
// generateUniqueSlug — base + 6-char random suffix, no retry loop
// seedDefaultRoles — loads permissions from DB by key, creates 6 roles
public OnboardingConflictException(String field, String code, String message) {
Wire this into GlobalExceptionHandler to return structured 409 response.
private String tenantId; // UUID (String for API consistency)
SignupController should now delegate to TenantOnboardingService. The OnboardingMeta captures request context:
Add missing DB-level constraints to catch any edge cases that application logic misses:
-- Ensure tenants.name is unique (case-insensitive via citext or lower index)
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_tenants_name_lower
-- Add company_code NOT NULL and unique constraint (should already exist)
ALTER COLUMN company_code SET NOT NULL;
ALTER COLUMN workspace_slug SET NOT NULL;
-- Partial index: only one ACTIVE/TRIAL subscription per tenant
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_one_active_subscription
-- Ensure users.email is globally unique (already enforced at app layer)
-- This is a safety net for the race condition case
-- Already exists as unique constraint on users table

TenantPersistenceService used REQUIRES_NEW to split the transaction. With the new design, this is no longer needed for the signup flow. TenantPersistenceService can remain for other uses (e.g. platform-admin tenant creation) but SignupService/OnboardingService should NOT use it.
tenantRepository.saveAndFlush(tenant) inside the outer @Transactional — flushes to session, does NOT commit
Two separate transactions: one for tenant, one for user
Single @Transactional boundary: saveAndFlush gives us the ID, all subsequent saves are in same session
TenantContext.set() and .clear() in try/finally inside service
TenantContext.set() and .clear() in try/finally inside onboard(), after saveAndFlush()
Every acceptance criterion requires a corresponding integration test using Testcontainers + PostgreSQL
Happy path: new tenant, new email, valid request
201 response, DB has tenant + user + subscription + 6 roles + audit log. Tenant status=ACTIVE.
409, field=ownerEmail, code=EMAIL_TAKEN. No tenant row in DB.
Duplicate email — case insensitive (Admin@TEST.com vs admin@test.com)
409, same as OB-02. Both uppercase and mixed case blocked.
409, field=businessName, code=NAME_TAKEN. No user row in DB.
DB write fails mid-transaction (simulate by throwing from subscriptionRepo.save)
500, no tenant row, no user row, no roles. Full rollback confirmed.
Concurrent signups with same business name (10 parallel requests)
Exactly 1 succeeds (201), all others get 409. DB has exactly 1 tenant with that name.
Exactly 1 succeeds, all others get 409. users table has exactly 1 row for that email.
Admin user can login after signup with companyCode
POST /api/auth/login with companyCode returns 200 with accessToken.
Admin user has TENANT_ADMIN role and can access /api/tenant/products
Trial subscription has correct end_date (+7 days from signup)
Subscription.endDate within 1 second of expected value.
6 default roles exist in DB after signup, each with correct permissions
DB query confirms role count = 6, TENANT_ADMIN has all permissions.
Audit log entry created with correct tenantId, action=SIGNUP
audit_logs table has 1 row with action=SIGNUP and matching tenantId.
workspaceSlug is URL-safe (no spaces, special chars, lowercase)
companyCode is 8 chars: 4 alpha prefix + 4 digits
Two tenants with similar business names get different slugs
Both slugs unique in DB due to random suffix.
TenantContext is cleared after signup (even on exception)
After a failed signup attempt, a subsequent valid signup succeeds — context not polluted.
Response body contains all required fields
companyCode, workspaceSlug, tenantId, adminUserId, plan, subscriptionStatus, trialEndsAt all present and non-null.
OnboardingConflictException thrown with correct field and code
TenantContext.getTenantId() returns null after onboard() throws
This feature is DONE when all of the following are true:
A successful signup creates: 1 tenant + 6 roles + 1 user + 1 subscription + audit log in a single atomic commit
A failed mid-flow signup leaves ZERO rows in tenants, users, roles, subscriptions tables
Duplicate email returns 409 with field-specific error before any DB write
Duplicate business name returns 409 with field-specific error before any DB write
10 concurrent signups with same email result in exactly 1 success
Admin user created during signup can immediately login and access tenant APIs
Admin user has TENANT_ADMIN role with all non-platform permissions
Trial subscription has 7-day duration from signup timestamp
workspaceSlug is globally unique and URL-safe
TenantContext is always cleared after onboarding attempt (success or failure)
All 20 integration tests pass in CI pipeline
No regression in existing platform tenant creation flow (TenantController.createTenant)
Before deploying this fix, audit the database for broken tenants (no admin user or no subscription):
LEFT JOIN users u ON u.tenant_id = t.id
LEFT JOIN subscriptions s ON s.tenant_id = t.id
For each broken tenant found: soft-delete the tenant (status=INACTIVE) and log it. Do NOT attempt to repair them — the data is incomplete and unpredictable.
Run audit SQL above. Record any broken tenants.
Deploy Flyway V23 migration (adds DB constraints).
Verify OB-01 happy path works in staging with real DB.
Verify OB-08 rollback works (inject fault in staging).
Deploy to production during low-traffic window.
Monitor error logs for 30 minutes post-deploy.
If a gradual rollout is preferred, add a system_config key:
Flip to true after staging validation. Remove the flag in the next sprint.
Create OnboardingResponse DTO + OnboardingResult + OnboardingMeta DTOs
Run audit SQL on staging DB, document broken tenants
These are the permissions that must exist in the database (seeded by Flyway or SeedService) before the onboarding service can assign them to roles:
